### Post by rahimveron on 2007-08-12
Hi guys i have to, on every boot up,switch on  my  swap partition by going to GParted. Why is that my swap is not on automatically on boot up ?
This is what i get from  etc/fstab
```
# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
#  -- This file has been automaticly generated by ntfs-config -- 
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>

proc /proc proc defaults 0 0
# Entry for /dev/sda6 :
UUID=8b9d6c63-39da-4f05-8b46-ad061ade46aa / ext3 defaults,errors=remount-ro 0 1
# Entry for /dev/sda1 :
UUID=9A1CD82A1CD80365 /media/sda1 ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_IN 0 1
# Entry for /dev/sda5 :
UUID=28DC017ADC014392 /media/sda5 ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_IN 0 1
# Entry for /dev/ !! UNKNOW DEVICE !! :
UUID=c6a48b88-4220-4f67-8b4c-1e03566718da none swap sw 0 0
/dev/scd0 /media/cdrom0 udf,iso9660 user,noauto 0 0
/dev/scd1 /media/cdrom1 udf,iso9660 user,noauto 0 0
/dev/fd0 /media/floppy0 auto rw,user,noauto 0 0
```

Here is my GParted window image after i swapon

### Post by ridgeland on 2007-08-12
I bet the error is UUID, try fstab with the path instead.

### Post by jgoguen on 2007-08-12
> **rahimveron said:**
> Hi guys i have to, on every boot up,switch on  my  swap partition by going to GParted. Why is that my swap is not on automatically on boot up ?
This is what i get from  etc/fstab
```
# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
#  -- This file has been automaticly generated by ntfs-config -- 
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>

proc /proc proc defaults 0 0
# Entry for /dev/sda6 :
UUID=8b9d6c63-39da-4f05-8b46-ad061ade46aa / ext3 defaults,errors=remount-ro 0 1
# Entry for /dev/sda1 :
UUID=9A1CD82A1CD80365 /media/sda1 ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_IN 0 1
# Entry for /dev/sda5 :
UUID=28DC017ADC014392 /media/sda5 ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_IN 0 1
# Entry for /dev/ !! UNKNOW DEVICE !! :
UUID=c6a48b88-4220-4f67-8b4c-1e03566718da none swap sw 0 0
/dev/scd0 /media/cdrom0 udf,iso9660 user,noauto 0 0
/dev/scd1 /media/cdrom1 udf,iso9660 user,noauto 0 0
/dev/fd0 /media/floppy0 auto rw,user,noauto 0 0
```Here is my GParted window image after i swapon


It doesn't seem like the UUID c6a48b88-4220-4f67-8b4c-1e03566718da can be found.  Do this:

0. Open a terminal
1. ls -l /dev/disk/by-uuid/ | grep sda7
Here you will see one line and it has something that looks like the UUIDs in /etc/fstab.  That's what we want, select it and copy it.  If there's nothing there, Ubuntu doesn't recognize that /dev/sda7 (your swap partition) has a unique ID, and we need to generate one.
2.  Edit /etc/fstab and replace 'c6a48b88-4220-4f67-8b4c-1e03566718da' with the UUID found in step 1.
3.  Save and exit, and reboot. When you come back into Ubuntu, swap should be working.

Of course, if the UUID you find is the same as the UUID in /etc/fstab, I'm not sure what the problem might be...

---

### Post by ridgeland on 2007-08-12
I got rid of all the UUIDs in my fstab.
I had problems like these and never saw anything of value from UUID.
When a new OS install on a new partition reformats the swap space it gets a new UUID, thus the old references are invalid.  This is a big negative is you ever install a second Linux OS.  I have several OSs and try more so to me UUID was just a bug.

---

2.  Edit /etc/fstab and replace 'c6a48b88-4220-4f67-8b4c-1e03566718da' with the UUID found in step 1.
3.  Save and exit, and reboot. When you come back into Ubuntu, swap should be working.

Of course, if the UUID you find is the same as the UUID in /etc/fstab, I'm not sure what the problem might be...

### Post by jgoguen on 2007-08-13
> **ridgeland said:**
> I got rid of all the UUIDs in my fstab.
I had problems like these and never saw anything of value from UUID.
When a new OS install on a new partition reformats the swap space it gets a new UUID, thus the old references are invalid.  This is a big negative is you ever install a second Linux OS.  I have several OSs and try more so to me UUID was just a bug.
Of course, don't forget that UUID is a huge benefit to people like me.  I have a couple external drives, and there's no guarantee they're detected in the same order each time.  At one point, I had to manually mount them after finding what their device name was.  Now, I can just put their UUID in /etc/fstab and know that it will always Just Work.  The same thing happens when I add a new drive sometimes, and UUID makes things so much easier for me.

### Post by ridgeland on 2007-08-13
At last!
Someone has told me something good about UUID.  Tell me more.
I've created /etc/udev/rules.d/10-local.rules
to set rules for automount of my camera as alway "Coolpix S4" and my card reader as always "PNY Compact Flash" but when I tried to set rules for my external drive it didn't work well.  It was easier to let it default to "Drive1" or whatever than to control it with the USB rules.
What happens when fstab expects UUID ExtDrive but it's not plugged in?  Any issues with hotplug after boot?

---

### Post by jgoguen on 2007-08-13
> **ridgeland said:**
> At last!
Someone has told me something good about UUID.  Tell me more.

UUIDs are Universally Unique Identifiers.  A partition has a new one generated every time a file system is created on it.  AFAIK, a simple format doesn't generate a new one, unless your format involves re-creating the partition.  They're unique to the partition regardless of its device name, so they can be used to help moving drives across installations.  Assuming of course the new installation supports UUIDs.  And you can't transfer your root file system like that since the new installation typically likes to fully format it.  I love my multiple partition setup :)  More info on using UUIDs in Ubuntu [here](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/UsingUUID).

> 
I've created /etc/usb/rules.d/10-local.rules to set rules for automount of my camera as alway "Coolpix S4" and my card reader as always "PNY Compact Flash" but when I tried to set rules for my external drive it didn't work well.  It was easier to let it default to "Drive1" or whatever than to control it with the USB rules.
I've never tried that method, since when I plug in a media device I don't much care for the mount point and I'm just dragging files onto or off of it.  So I've also never tried UUID with a camera or card reader, although I would imagine it would work just as well.

> What happens when fstab expects UUID ExtDrive but it's not plugged in?  Any issues with hotplug after boot?
If the drive isn't there, it gracefully fails that mount and moves on to the next one.  It's happened before where one of my external drives wasn't plugged in, and I never even noticed until it was time for backups and the backups barfed.  But that was by design, if the mount point isn't there I don't want the backup completing until it's there.  As for hotplug, it just isn't quite there yet.  It's easy to add in though.  Open a terminal and edit /etc/udev/rules.d/95-hal.rules:
```
sudo nano /etc/udev/rules.d/95-hal.rules
```
Add this line at the very end:
```
SUBSYSTEM=="block", RUN+="/bin/mount -a"
```
Save and exit, and restart udev:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/udev restart
```
After that, hotplugging should Just Work.

---

### Post by ridgeland on 2007-08-13
Thanks.
With the udev rules I solved a problem of the camera mounting as rwx for me but only me.  That caused problems with NFS sharing.  Now the camera and card reader copy files across with mode 777.  So my wife can also edit shared directories (photos and music mostly) via NFS.  Now the camera always mounts to /mount/CoolpixS4.  This approach can solve the issue you solved by using UUID.  Fixed mount points means the order mounted doesn't matter.

I use an External Drive for backups, but only once a month.  I thought with the udev rules I could get a neat name like "IOMega Drive" rather than "Drive" but it made the mount harder so I'm happy with just "Drive" for a label.  The udev rules worked great when the ExtDrive was already plugged in at boot, not so well if it was hot plugged later.  
Still none of this used UUID just the product's name.
Seems both ways get about the same results.  Good with external drives connected before boot, poor with hot plugged devices.

I do understand that if /dev/sda4 gets moved to a new place like /dev/sdb4 then using UUID would make the move easier.  fstab would still find it and install it as before.  Useful for corporations, not so much for me.

I learned about udev rules from this Ubuntu Forum post:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=168221](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=168221)

### Post by jgoguen on 2007-08-13
One of the greatest blessings of Linux - there's usually more than one way to get the same result, each with their own high and low points, which typically provides everyone with a way to do things that does exactly what they want.  In Windows...not so easy, and order matters...
